theme: two hidden tuning forks in one box
title: Two Tuning Forks in a Box: Extrapolating a Coupled-Mode Resonance
strategies: trivial: predict a constant amplitude (mean of the training |X1| column), ignoring w entirely -> reproduces the checker baseline ~0.1

greedy: collapse the lock-in data to a scalar amplitude (discard the phase/quadrature channel entirely) and fit the textbook single driven-damped-oscillator Lorentzian A=c/sqrt((w0^2-w^2)^2+(gamma0*w)^2) by bounded nonlinear least squares. Matches the smooth, featureless training tail well, but a single Lorentzian has only ONE pole pair: extrapolated it predicts one resonance peak and a 1/w^2 high-frequency roll-off, where the true box holds two coupled oscillators with two split peaks (avoided crossing) and a 1/w^4 roll-off -- the trap on every instance, since the tail alone never shows a peak either way

strong: commit to the physical two-mode transfer function CLASS and fit ALL FIVE physical constants (Om1,Om2,g1,g2,kc) directly against the phase-resolved (Xre,Xim) data via bounded multi-start nonlinear least squares. A single Lorentzian structurally cannot represent a second natural frequency no matter how it is tuned; the two-mode form can, so even an imperfect fit to noisy tail-only data reproduces both resonance peaks, the avoided-crossing splitting between them, and the correct 1/w^4 asymptote once extrapolated -- because the RIGHT model class was chosen, not because more data was available

headroom above strong: better global optimization of the same 5-parameter physical model (e.g. a proper Levenberg-Marquardt/basin-hopping schedule, or reweighting residuals by local curvature) to push the recovered constants toward the training-noise floor
